title: Do Blue Light Glasses Actually Do Anything? What the Research Says
description: Honest look at whether blue light glasses work for eye strain, sleep, and screen fatigue. We cover the latest research, what optometrists say, and who might benefit.
tldr: Blue light glasses probably won't prevent eye damage or cure digital eye strain — most strain comes from how you use screens, not the light itself. But some people find the slight warmth of blue-light-filtered lenses more comfortable, and wearing them in the evening may help with sleep if you're sensitive to screen light.

tldrStats.label: Of screen blue light vs. sunlight

definition.text: Eyeglasses with lenses that filter or absorb a portion of blue-violet light (approximately 380–450 nm wavelength) emitted by digital screens, LED lighting, and sunlight. Available as prescription or non-prescription, typically with a slight yellow tint.

sections.h2: The Blue Light Glasses Debate, Simplified
sections.content:
Blue light glasses became a massive trend during the pandemic when screen time skyrocketed. The marketing was compelling: screens emit harmful blue light, blue light causes eye strain and damage, these glasses block it. Simple story. But the science tells a more nuanced one.

Multiple peer-reviewed studies — including a large 2023 Cochrane review — found insufficient evidence that blue light filtering lenses reduce eye strain, improve visual performance, or protect retinal health. The Canadian Association of Optometrists doesn't recommend them as essential for screen users.

That doesn't mean they're useless. It means the benefits are more limited and more individual than the marketing suggests.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

sections.h2: What Blue Light Glasses Actually Filter
sections.content:
Most blue light glasses filter 15–40% of blue-violet light in the 400–450 nm range. Higher-filter lenses have a more noticeable yellow tint. Some premium coatings are nearly clear while still filtering some blue light.

Here's the thing: your phone or laptop emits far less blue light than the sun. Walking outside for 15 minutes exposes your eyes to more blue light than eight hours of screen time. If blue light from screens were truly damaging, sunlight would be catastrophic — and humans have been dealing with sunlight for a while now.

The amount of blue light reaching your retina from screens is simply too low to cause the kind of damage that concerned early researchers studying high-intensity blue light in lab settings.

sections.h2: Digital Eye Strain: The Real Culprit
sections.content:
If your eyes feel tired, dry, or strained after long screen sessions, blue light isn't the primary cause. Digital eye strain (also called computer vision syndrome) mainly results from:

  • Reduced blinking: You blink 66% less when staring at screens, causing dryness
  • Sustained close focus: Your eye muscles fatigue from holding focus at one distance for hours
  • Poor ergonomics: Screen too close, too far, too high, or too low
  • Incorrect prescription: An outdated Rx or lenses not optimized for your working distance

The 20-20-20 rule helps more than any lens coating: every 20 minutes, look at something 20 feet away for 20 seconds. Getting an up-to-date prescription from an eye exam is even more effective.

sections.h2: Where Blue Light Glasses Might Actually Help
sections.content:
Sleep quality: This is where the evidence is more supportive. Blue light in the 460–480 nm range does suppress melatonin production. If you use screens within 2 hours of bedtime and have trouble falling asleep, blue light glasses (or your phone's night mode) can help your brain wind down.

Subjective comfort: Some people report that the slight warmth of blue-filtered lenses feels more comfortable during extended screen use. Is it placebo? Possibly. Does it matter if it helps? Not really.

Migraine sensitivity: People with migraines or photophobia sometimes find that filtering certain light wavelengths reduces triggers. This is a specific medical use case worth discussing with your optometrist.

sections.h2: Should You Add Blue Light Coating to Your Glasses?
sections.content:
At $30–$80 as an add-on to prescription lenses, blue light coating is relatively low-cost for peace of mind. It won't hurt anything, and the slight tint is barely noticeable in modern coatings.

If you're ordering new prescription glasses, it's reasonable to include blue light filtering alongside anti-reflective coating — especially if you work on screens all day or use them before bed. Just don't expect it to be a magic fix for eye strain.

What will actually fix screen-related eye discomfort: a current prescription, proper screen distance (arm's length), adequate room lighting, and regular breaks. If you haven't had an eye exam recently, that's the better investment. Many insurance plans cover annual or biennial exams in Alberta.

sections.h2: What Optometrists Actually Recommend for Screen Users
sections.content:
Rather than relying on blue light glasses alone, most Canadian optometrists suggest a combination approach:

  • Follow the 20-20-20 rule consistently
  • Keep screens at arm's length and slightly below eye level
  • Use artificial tears if your eyes feel dry
  • Ensure your prescription includes your screen working distance
  • Adjust screen brightness to match your room lighting
  • Consider dedicated computer glasses with an intermediate-distance prescription if you're over 40

If you're experiencing persistent eye strain or headaches, book an eye exam rather than buying blue light glasses as a band-aid solution. The strain might be from an outdated prescription, early astigmatism, or dry eye disease — all of which need professional attention.

The best way to evaluate frame styles is to try them on in person. Photos and online try-on tools give a rough idea, but they can't replicate how a frame feels on your face — the weight distribution, the temple pressure, and how the nose bridge sits. These comfort factors matter more than aesthetics for glasses you'll wear 12+ hours a day.

sections.h2: How to Choose the Right Lenses
sections.content:
Choosing the right lenses starts with understanding your prescription and daily visual demands. Your optometrist will assess your refraction, measure your pupillary distance, and discuss your lifestyle needs — whether you spend most of your day on screens, driving, reading, or working outdoors. Each of these activities benefits from specific lens designs and coatings.

Lens material matters too. Standard CR-39 plastic is affordable and optically clear, but higher prescriptions benefit from high-index materials (1.60, 1.67, or 1.74) that produce thinner, lighter lenses. Polycarbonate and Trivex offer impact resistance for active lifestyles and children's glasses. The right combination of material, design, and coatings gives you the clearest, most comfortable vision possible.

The best way to navigate these choices is with professional guidance. A comprehensive eye exam at Charm Optical includes a detailed discussion of lens options tailored to your specific needs. The opticians at Charm Optical in Edmonton can walk you through each option side by side, and many lens types are available for same-day glasses at Charm Optical, so you don't have to wait days for your new glasses.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. faqs.q: Are blue light glasses a scam?
faqs.a: They're not a scam, but they're oversold. They do filter some blue light, and some people find them comfortable. The science just doesn't support the big claims about preventing eye damage or curing digital eye strain. They're a minor comfort feature, not a medical necessity.

faqs.q: Do optometrists recommend blue light glasses?
faqs.a: Most Canadian optometrists say blue light glasses are optional, not essential. They'll offer the coating as an add-on if you want it, but they emphasize proper prescription, screen habits, and the 20-20-20 rule as more effective solutions for screen-related discomfort.

faqs.q: Can blue light glasses help with sleep?
faqs.a: Potentially yes. Blue light in the 460–480 nm range does suppress melatonin. Wearing blue light glasses or using night mode on devices for 1–2 hours before bed may help you fall asleep faster, especially if you're sensitive to screen light.

faqs.q: Do blue light glasses have a yellow tint?
faqs.a: Some do, some don't. Higher-filtration lenses have a noticeable warm tint. Modern premium blue light coatings are nearly clear, filtering a smaller percentage of blue light with minimal colour distortion.

faqs.q: Should I get blue light glasses for my kids?
faqs.a: For children, limiting screen time and ensuring regular outdoor play (which helps prevent myopia progression) is far more effective than blue light glasses. If your child uses screens regularly, a comprehensive eye exam to check for uncorrected vision problems is the better first step.
